candidates.title: Distributed login identities cause unbounded throttle state and per-request linear work
reproductionSteps: Insert failures for many distinct source/account keys through the public-login throttle path.
Invoke the request-path expiry sweep as the retained key count grows.
Measure retained entries, heap growth, and sweep time.
candidates.expectedBehavior: Attacker-controlled login identities cannot create unbounded process state or make every login perform work linear in all retained identities.
candidates.actualBehavior: A process-global Map has no cardinality ceiling and pruneExpired traverses the full Map on every login request.
candidates.userImpact: Distributed probing can increase memory use and login latency for all users.
candidates.frequency: Deterministic under sustained distinct-key traffic; direct validation used 10,000 account keys.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: Authentication availability degradation; no data loss demonstrated.
candidates.rootCause: The throttle has neither bounded admission/eviction nor a bounded cleanup budget.
evidence: Scan validation observed 3,474,752 bytes of heap growth and increasing sweep segments.
candidates.proposedDisposition: Add bounded deterministic eviction and bounded/periodic cleanup with regression coverage.

regression.before: The cap and bounded-sweep tests failed against the baseline; review then showed unconditional capacity eviction could also discard an active lockout.
regression.after: The throttle suite covers the cap, fixed-size sweep, and a locked identity that remains enforced while attacker-controlled keys flood capacity.

fix.summary: Cap retained counters at a configurable 4,096 by default, maintain an O(1) unlocked-entry eviction index that pins active lockouts, decline new admission when every retained identity is locked, and inspect at most 64 entries per request-path prune.

candidates.title: Active-account enumeration through unequal bcrypt work
reproductionSteps: Send wrong-password login requests for an enabled account and an unknown account below the lockout threshold.
Compare repeated response-time distributions.
candidates.expectedBehavior: Generic invalid-credential responses do comparable password-verification work regardless of account existence or enabled state.
candidates.actualBehavior: Reserved, missing, and disabled identities return before bcrypt while enabled identities perform bcrypt comparison, producing separable latency.
candidates.userImpact: An anonymous actor can classify active staff login identities for targeted phishing or credential attacks.
candidates.frequency: Deterministic on the loopback build within eight samples; WAN jitter may require more windows.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: Account-existence confidentiality loss; no password or session disclosure.
candidates.rootCause: verifyCredentials uses identity-dependent early returns instead of a constant-work dummy hash path.
evidence: Original-route timing validation produced strictly separated local distributions.
candidates.proposedDisposition: Always perform one bcrypt comparison using a fixed dummy hash when no usable account hash exists.

regression.before: The new bcrypt-call-count regression failed for reserved, unknown, and disabled identities.
regression.after: All identity classes execute one bcrypt comparison and the full auth suite passes.

fix.summary: Use a fixed cost-10 dummy hash whenever no usable credential hash may be consulted, while retaining the generic public failure response.

candidates.title: Same-millisecond password rotation could theoretically equal a session timestamp
reproductionSteps: Attempt to force credential verification, password rotation, and session issuance into the same millisecond.
Replay the resulting cookie after the rotation watermark.
candidates.expectedBehavior: Sessions proven with an old credential do not survive password rotation.
candidates.actualBehavior: The proposed equality could not be reached through the supported path: blocking bcrypt introduced a measured 58-62 ms ordering gap.
candidates.userImpact: No realistic supported exploit path was established.
candidates.rootCause: The candidate assumed same-millisecond ordering that the synchronous credential-work path does not permit.
evidence: Central validation and attack-path analysis rejected the timing premise.
candidates.proposedDisposition: No change; retain as rejected evidence distinct from the independently confirmed in-flight login race.

candidates.title: In-flight old-password verification can mint a post-rotation session
candidates.affectedWorkflow: Login racing password reset or self-service password change
reproductionSteps: Begin a valid login and pause after its credential row is read.
Rotate the same user's password and session watermark.
Resume bcrypt verification and allow the login route to issue a new session.
Use the newly issued cookie against a protected route.
candidates.expectedBehavior: A credential rotation invalidates both existing sessions and in-flight authentication based on the old credential state.
candidates.actualBehavior: Credential verification returns a user detached from its credential version; the route stamps the resumed session after the rotation watermark, so it is accepted.
candidates.userImpact: An actor holding the old password can retain access despite a concurrent recovery rotation.
candidates.frequency: Deterministic when the validated race ordering is forced; requires overlap with a rotation.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: Authentication recovery boundary bypass for the same account.
candidates.rootCause: Session issuance does not prove that the verified password hash/version still matches the live user row.
candidates.proposedDisposition: Carry a credential version from verification and compare it atomically before session issuance.

regression.before: A session created after a concurrent rotation remained valid because issuedAt was newer than the watermark.
regression.after: Login stores the credential version read with the verified hash and stale-version replay is rejected.

fix.summary: Bind every new session to the exact sessions_valid_after value read alongside the password hash; preserve a compatibility path for legacy cookies.

candidates.title: Anonymous failures can lock a chosen staff account out of new logins
reproductionSteps: Submit the configured number of wrong passwords for a known staff email from an anonymous source.
Attempt a correct login for that account from a different source before the lock expires.
candidates.expectedBehavior: Anonymous traffic is throttled without letting one source deny a chosen account access across all sources.
candidates.actualBehavior: The account-key counter is global across sources, so ten anonymous failures lock the victim account for five minutes.
candidates.userImpact: An attacker knowing a staff email can repeatedly deny that user fresh logins.
candidates.frequency: Deterministic at the configured threshold and duration.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: Authentication availability loss; existing sessions remain unaffected.
candidates.rootCause: The throttle treats attacker-driven account failures as a global hard lock rather than a source-scoped spray signal.
candidates.proposedDisposition: Retain source-IP enforcement while redesigning account abuse tracking so anonymous actors cannot impose a victim-wide hard lock.

regression.before: A correct password received 429 after attacker-driven account failures.
regression.after: Wrong guesses remain throttled while the legitimate correct credential can recover the account.

fix.summary: Apply the account-wide lock only after failed verification; retain the independent source-IP lock before credential work.

candidates.title: Leading whitespace bypasses formula neutralization in Trends CSV exports
candidates.affectedWorkflow: Trends CSV export opened in spreadsheet software
reproductionSteps: Store administrator-controlled KPI text beginning with LF or mixed whitespace followed by a spreadsheet formula marker.
Export Trends CSV.
Open the file in Apple Numbers 14.5.
candidates.expectedBehavior: Every exported text cell whose first meaningful character is =, +, -, or @ is inert in supported spreadsheet consumers.
candidates.actualBehavior: The CSV helper misses LF and mixed whitespace prefixes; Apple Numbers evaluated the validated cells as formulas.
candidates.userImpact: A staff member opening an export can execute a planted spreadsheet formula in that consumer.
candidates.frequency: Deterministic for the tested prefixes in Apple Numbers; LibreOffice preserved strings.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: Downstream formula execution; stronger network, command, or data effects were not demonstrated.
candidates.rootCause: Formula-prefix detection models only direct markers, tab, carriage return, or ASCII-space runs.
evidence: Apple Numbers 14.5 validation evaluated probes to 2, 4, and 6.
candidates.proposedDisposition: Neutralize a formula marker after any leading whitespace/control run, then RFC-4180 quote.

regression.before: Mixed leading whitespace/control characters exposed formula markers in exported text.
regression.after: The shared CSV serializer neutralizes all covered prefixes and the export suite passes.

fix.summary: Scan past any leading whitespace/control run before testing the formula marker, while preserving direct tab and carriage-return hardening.

candidates.title: Mutable OSV-Scanner image can falsify a required dependency-security gate
reproductionSteps: Inspect the OSV scanner fallback image reference.
Resolve the version tag through the registry.
Compare the mutable source reference with the immutable digest used at runtime.
candidates.expectedBehavior: CI executes a repository-reviewed, immutable scanner artifact.
candidates.actualBehavior: The Docker fallback executes a version tag without an image digest, allowing registry retargeting without a repository diff.
candidates.userImpact: An upstream compromise can falsify or deny the dependency-security gate within the container's bounded authority.
candidates.frequency: Every Docker-fallback CI execution; exploitation requires publisher or registry compromise.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: Supply-chain integrity loss; no runner takeover was established.
candidates.rootCause: scripts/run-osv-scanner.mjs authenticates a mutable label rather than immutable content.
candidates.proposedDisposition: Pin the validated multi-platform digest and enforce the reference in policy tests.

regression.before: The policy test rejected the mutable OSV tag-only reference.
regression.after: The policy test and live OSV dependency scan pass with the reviewed digest.

candidates.title: Mutable Gitleaks container tag weakens Secret Scan integrity
reproductionSteps: Inspect the Gitleaks fallback image reference.
Resolve the version tag through the registry.
Compare the mutable source reference with the immutable digest used at runtime.
candidates.expectedBehavior: CI executes a repository-reviewed, immutable secret scanner artifact.
candidates.actualBehavior: The Docker fallback executes a version tag without a digest.
candidates.userImpact: An upstream compromise can falsify or deny the Secret Scan job within the container's bounded authority.
candidates.frequency: Every Docker-fallback CI execution; exploitation requires publisher or registry compromise.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: Supply-chain integrity loss; no runner takeover was established.
candidates.rootCause: scripts/run-gitleaks.mjs authenticates a mutable label rather than immutable content.
candidates.proposedDisposition: Pin the validated multi-platform digest and enforce the reference in policy tests.

regression.before: The policy test rejected the mutable Gitleaks tag-only reference.
regression.after: The policy test and full 110-commit Gitleaks scan pass with the reviewed digest.

candidates.title: Cross-site login can replace the visitor's local session
reproductionSteps: Cause a browser to submit valid attacker credentials to the login endpoint.
Inspect the session and authorization available afterward.
candidates.expectedBehavior: A login creates only the authenticated session represented by the submitted credentials.
candidates.actualBehavior: The browser becomes logged in as the submitted account, but the attacker receives no victim cookie, data, or additional authority.
candidates.userImpact: No boundary-crossing impact was demonstrated within the product's same-site and authorization model.
candidates.frequency: The session swap is reproducible; harmful impact is not.
candidates.rootCause: The candidate equated a local browser session replacement with transfer of victim authority to the attacker.
evidence: Real-browser validation found no attacker access to the victim session or data.
candidates.proposedDisposition: No security fix; retain the rejected attack-path evidence.

candidates.title: Operator-provided bootstrap passwords can be intentionally weak
reproductionSteps: As a trusted operator, provide a trivial bootstrap password through the documented secret channel.
Provision the account and attempt login.
candidates.expectedBehavior: Bootstrap secrets remain operator-controlled, unlogged, and force rotation before application access.
candidates.actualBehavior: A privileged operator can deliberately choose a weak secret, while secure random defaults, secret-channel guidance, and forced rotation remain intact.
candidates.userImpact: No lower-privileged exploit path exists without a trusted operator making the unsafe choice.
candidates.frequency: Only when an operator deliberately supplies a weak password.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: Operational policy risk, not a confirmed application boundary failure.
candidates.rootCause: The candidate treated password-strength policy as an existing enforced product contract.
evidence: Secure defaults and must-change enforcement remain present.
candidates.proposedDisposition: No stabilization fix or issue; any password-complexity policy is separate product/security-policy work.

candidates.title: Anonymous oversized login JSON is parsed before throttle enforcement
reproductionSteps: Send schema-invalid login JSON bodies between 8 MiB and 20 MiB to the production build.
Repeat requests and observe status, Retry-After, latency, and process health.
candidates.expectedBehavior: Anonymous login work is bounded before parsing or by a proven production ingress limit.
candidates.actualBehavior: The application parses bodies through 20 MiB and schema-invalid requests return before throttle accounting; bounded local concurrency slowed health checks but did not cause an outage.
candidates.userImpact: Potential process memory/CPU pressure if production ingress accepts enough concurrent large bodies.
candidates.frequency: Local parsing is deterministic; production outage frequency is unproven.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: Potential availability degradation; no repeatable outage or data loss established.
candidates.rootCause: req.json executes before an application body-size bound or throttle admission, while the effective Fly edge ceiling is undocumented in the repository.
evidence: 20,971,547-byte body reached post-parse 400; four concurrent bodies caused bounded slowdown without outage.
candidates.proposedDisposition: Retain as a proof gap, not a confirmed defect; do not create a GitHub bug issue unless production reachability and repeatable impact are confirmed.

candidates.title: Successful login resets the shared source-IP password-spraying budget
reproductionSteps: From one source IP, submit failures against several accounts while staying below each account threshold.
Log in successfully to any controlled account from that IP.
Continue the spray and observe the IP failure budget.
candidates.expectedBehavior: A success for one account does not erase aggregate failures against other accounts from the same source.
candidates.actualBehavior: The success path calls clearFailures for both account and IP keys, resetting the shared IP budget.
candidates.userImpact: An attacker with one valid account can repeatedly reset the main source-level spraying control.
candidates.frequency: Deterministic on every successful login from the spraying IP.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: Reduced credential-attack resistance across staff accounts.
candidates.rootCause: Account success and aggregate source abuse are conflated in the same cleanup operation.
evidence: Original-route sequence proved the post-success IP budget reset.
candidates.proposedDisposition: Clear only the successful account's failures; preserve the independent IP budget.

regression.before: A successful login erased failures accumulated against other accounts from the same source.
regression.after: Account success clears only that account counter; the source-IP spray budget remains.

fix.summary: Remove IP-counter clearing from successful authentication while retaining successful-account recovery.

candidates.title: Stale self-service reauthentication can overwrite a concurrent administrator reset
candidates.affectedWorkflow: Password recovery and forced self-service password change
reproductionSteps: Begin self-service current-password verification and pause before its write.
Reset the same account password as an administrator.
Resume the self-service request and attempt both replacement passwords.
candidates.expectedBehavior: An administrator reset invalidates stale self-service proof and cannot be overwritten by it.
candidates.actualBehavior: The route verifies one credential row snapshot and later updates by user id only, so the stale replacement wins.
candidates.userImpact: A compromised same-account foothold can undo administrator recovery and retain durable credential control.
candidates.frequency: Deterministic with the validated ordering; requires a concurrent reset window.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: Credential recovery integrity failure for the same account.
candidates.rootCause: Reauthentication is not bound to the password hash/version consumed by the subsequent update.
candidates.proposedDisposition: Use a compare-and-swap password update against the verified credential version.

regression.before: A stale self-service request could overwrite an administrator reset after reauthentication.
regression.after: The stale write loses the compare-and-swap, returns 409, and destroys its session.

fix.summary: Update the password only where the current hash still equals the hash just reauthenticated; preserve atomic watermark rotation on success.

candidates.title: VS Code MCP fallback can resolve registry code only after multiple explicit preconditions
reproductionSteps: Trust the repository in VS Code.
Explicitly start the MCP server with local bundles unavailable and network enabled.
Assume registry or publisher compromise.
candidates.expectedBehavior: Normal repository use prefers the checked local bundle and does not silently execute a remote fallback.
candidates.actualBehavior: The evidenced host used its local bundle; the proposed registry path required trust, explicit start, missing bundles, network access, and upstream compromise.
candidates.userImpact: No realistic default-path exploit was established.
candidates.frequency: 0 on the validated host; only under the full stated precondition chain.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: None established for the supported validated path.
candidates.rootCause: The candidate omitted strong user-action, environment, and local-bundle controls from its reachability claim.
candidates.proposedDisposition: No stabilization change; keep distinct from the independently reachable OpenCode @latest fallback.

candidates.title: Mutable OpenCode MCP package fallback executes unreviewed registry code
reproductionSteps: Run the checked OpenCode MCP command where the preferred local bundle path is unavailable.
Observe npx resolve @inkeep/open-knowledge@latest and execute it.
candidates.expectedBehavior: Repository-triggered developer tooling executes only local or immutable reviewed code and fails closed when it is unavailable.
candidates.actualBehavior: The fallback runs an unversioned @latest package from the registry.
candidates.userImpact: A compromised publisher or registry can execute code with developer-tool privileges when the fallback is reached.
candidates.frequency: Every fallback startup; requires the local path to be absent and registry resolution to succeed.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: Developer workstation code execution and repository/credential exposure within tool authority.
candidates.rootCause: opencode.json prioritizes availability over provenance with an enabled mutable network fallback.
candidates.proposedDisposition: Remove the @latest fallback and fail closed with a clear local-bundle requirement.

regression.before: The policy test found enabled npx and @latest registry fallback text.
regression.after: The configuration accepts only installed OK Desktop bundles and otherwise exits 127.

fix.summary: Remove all registry-executing fallbacks and fail closed with a clear local OK Desktop requirement.

candidates.title: Bcrypt ignores password bytes after its effective 72-byte prefix
reproductionSteps: Create two long passwords sharing the same first 72 bytes and differing only afterward.
Compare their bcrypt verification behavior.
candidates.expectedBehavior: Documented credential semantics remain secure under realistic attacker knowledge.
candidates.actualBehavior: The synthetic strings compare equivalently, but exploitation requires full knowledge of the effective 72-byte prefix, which retains ample entropy.
candidates.userImpact: No practical lower-privileged bypass was established.
candidates.frequency: Deterministic bcrypt property; harmful use requires complete prefix knowledge.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: None established under the supported threat model.
candidates.rootCause: The candidate treated a documented bcrypt input limit as a standalone authentication bypass.
evidence: Central attack-path analysis rejected practical reportability.
candidates.proposedDisposition: No stabilization fix; retain the explicit assumption and avoid overstating the primitive.

candidates.title: Password update sinks share bcrypt's 72-byte effective-prefix semantics
candidates.affectedWorkflow: Administrator reset and self-service password change
reproductionSteps: Set long replacement passwords with a shared 72-byte prefix through both update paths.
Compare authentication using a suffix-different value.
candidates.expectedBehavior: Both password update paths use consistent supported hashing semantics without a realistic bypass.
candidates.actualBehavior: Both sinks share bcrypt semantics, but exploitation still requires exact knowledge of every effective prefix byte.
candidates.userImpact: No practical boundary failure was established.
candidates.frequency: Deterministic bcrypt property; harmful use requires full prefix knowledge.
candidates.rootCause: The candidate duplicated the same non-exploitable primitive at two write sinks.
evidence: Central attack-path analysis rejected practical reportability.
candidates.proposedDisposition: No separate fix; record as the same expected bcrypt semantic evaluated in CAN-016.

candidates.title: A copied bearer cookie can be replayed until revocation
reproductionSteps: Copy an exact active session cookie.
Replay it from a second client before and after supported account-wide revocation events.
candidates.expectedBehavior: Bearer cookies authorize their holder until expiration or an account-wide revocation event invalidates them.
candidates.actualBehavior: The copied cookie works before revocation; password, role, status, or deletion changes revoke it through the durable watermark/user-row controls.
candidates.userImpact: No new exploit beyond prior theft of the exact bearer credential was established.
candidates.frequency: Expected for a valid stolen bearer cookie; revocation tests pass for all supported account changes.
candidates.dataLossOrSecurityRisk: Theft of an active cookie remains inherently sensitive; no missing revocation control was found.
candidates.rootCause: The candidate treated ordinary bearer replay after credential theft as proof of a session-management defect.
candidates.proposedDisposition: No change; retain the documented bearer/session revocation model.
